reasoning.step: Define the model and notation used throughout: let $d$ be the spatial dimension with $d=2+\epsilon$ where $\epsilon$ is a small positive parameter; let $n$ be the number of spin components with $n>2$; let $T$ denote the absolute temperature (in energy units); let $a$ denote the coefficient of the short-range kinetic term proportional to $k^{2}$ in Fourier space; let $b$ denote the coefficient of the long-range kinetic term proportional to $k^{\sigma}$ in Fourier space; let $\sigma$ be the LR exponent with the paper treating the case $2-\sigma=O(\epsilon)$; and let $\eta$ denote the anomalous-dimension exponent that enters the RG field rescaling $S_{\alpha}'(\vec{q}')=2^{-(d+2-\eta)/2}S_{\alpha}(\vec{q})$, where $S_{\alpha}(\vec{q})$ are the $(n-1)$ independent Fourier components of the spin field after eliminating one component via the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{n}S_{i}^{2}(\vec{x})=1$.

Write the effective Hamiltonian (energy functional divided by $-T$) in Fourier space for the $(n-1)$ fields $S_{\alpha}(\vec{k})$ (with $\alpha=1,\ldots,n-1$) after eliminating the $n$th component and expanding the square root to the necessary order in $S^{2}$ (the authors note that $\langle S^{2}\rangle=O(\epsilon)$ so higher terms can be neglected). The quadratic part in Fourier space is $$ -3c=\sum_{\alpha=1}^{n-1}\int\frac{d^{d}k}{(2\pi)^{d}}\frac{a k^{2}+b k^{\sigma}}{T}\,S_{\alpha}(\vec{k})S_{\alpha}(-\vec{k})+\cdots, $$ where the ellipsis denotes quartic and higher interaction terms generated by the elimination of $S_{n}$ and the expansion of the square root.

Define the RG transformation used: integrate out modes with wave vectors in the shell $1/2<q<1$ (where the unit of inverse length is set by the short-distance cutoff) and then rescale momenta by $q' = 2 q \in [0,1]$ while rescaling fields according to $S_{\alpha}'(\vec{q}')=2^{-(d+2-\eta)/2}S_{\alpha}(\vec{q})$. The fixed point value of $\eta$ is chosen so that repeated application of this RG transformation produces a nontrivial fixed point Hamiltonian rather than driving parameters to trivial limits.

State the diagrammatic recursion relations for the running couplings $(a/T)$ and $(b/T)$ obtained to first order in $\epsilon$ from integrating out the shell and rescaling; these relations are the basis for locating fixed points. The authors summarize the result in the form (with all momentum dependence shown explicitly as factors of $k^{2}$ and $k^{\sigma}$): $$ \bigg(\frac{a}{T}\bigg)'\,k^{2}+\bigg(\frac{b}{T}\bigg)'\,k^{\sigma} =2^{2-\sigma}\eta\left(\frac{1}{4}\frac{a}{T}k^{2}+\frac{1}{2^{\sigma}}\frac{b}{T}k^{\sigma}+\frac{1}{4}\frac{k^{2}}{2\pi}\ln2\right), $$ and the companion relation (which retains more factors of $\epsilon$ and $\eta$): $$ \bigg(\frac{a}{T}\bigg)'\,k^{2}+\bigg(\frac{b}{T}\bigg)'\,k^{\sigma} =2^{2-\epsilon-2\eta}\bigg(\frac{1}{4}\frac{a}{T}k^{2}+\frac{1}{2^{\sigma}}\frac{b}{T}k^{\sigma}+(\eta-1)\frac{\frac{1}{4}ak^{2}+\frac{1}{2^{\sigma}}bk^{\sigma}}{a+b}\frac{\ln2}{2\pi}+\frac{1}{4}k^{2}\frac{\ln2}{2\pi}\bigg). $$ These recursion relations were obtained by evaluating the diagrams shown by the authors (the diagrammatic rules and contributing diagrams are summarized in the paper's figure depicting the first-order diagrams). Fig. 1

Identify the short-range (SR) fixed-point candidate by setting the long-range coupling to zero at the fixed point, i.e., $b^{*}=0$, and solving for $(T/a)^{*}$ and $\eta$ self-consistently from the recursion relations specialized to $b=0$. With $b=0$ the authors obtain the SR fixed-point conditions $$ b^{*}=0,\qquad \left(\frac{T}{a}\right)^{*}=\frac{2\pi\,\epsilon}{\,n-2\,}, $$ and the corresponding anomalous-dimension value $$ \eta_{\mathrm{SR}}=\frac{\epsilon}{\,n-2\,}. $$ These expressions are presented by the authors as the fixed-point coordinates to first order in $\epsilon$ for $n>2$ and $d=2+\epsilon$.

Explain how the SR fixed-point results arise from the recursion relations: substituting $b=0$ into the recursion relations and requiring invariance under the RG transformation (fixed point condition) produces the algebraic equation for $T/a$ that yields $(T/a)^{*}=2\pi\epsilon/(n-2)$ and simultaneously fixes $\eta$ at $\eta_{\mathrm{SR}}=\epsilon/(n-2)$ to the order calculated. The authors also note that these values agree with prior low-temperature (d=2+ϵ) calculations for vector spins (references cited for earlier related calculations). [5]

conclusion.title: Short-range fixed point coordinates and anomalous dimension near d=2

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: Within the same low-temperature RG (d=2+ϵ) framework, the short-range (SR) fixed point is found to satisfy $b^{*}=0$ (vanishing long-range coupling) and the SR fixed-point ratio of temperature to the short-range stiffness is $$ \left(\frac{T}{a}\right)^{*} \;=\; \frac{2\pi\,\epsilon}{\,n-2\,}, $$ and the anomalous-dimension (field rescaling) exponent at this SR fixed point is, to leading order in $ϵ$, $$ \eta_{SR} \;=\; \frac{\epsilon}{\,n-2\,}, $$ where $d=2+\epsilon$, $n>2$, $a$ is the coefficient of the $k^{2}$ term and $T$ is the temperature in energy units; the exponent $\eta$ is defined by the field rescaling factor $S'(\vec{q}')=2^{-(d+2-\eta)/2}S(\vec{q})$ used in the RG transformation.

reasoning.step: Restate the context and notation needed for the LR fixed point derivation: $d=2+\epsilon$ with $\epsilon$ small, $n>2$, $a$ is the coefficient of the $k^{2}$ term, $b$ is the coefficient of the $k^{\sigma}$ term, $\sigma$ is the LR exponent with $2-\sigma=O(\epsilon)$, and $\eta$ is the RG anomalous-dimension exponent entering the field rescaling $S_{\alpha}'(\vec{q}')=2^{-(d+2-\eta)/2}S_{\alpha}(\vec{q})$.

Start from the same recursion relations for the running couplings $(a/T)$ and $(b/T)$ obtained by the shell integration and rescaling (these relations are the ones reproduced earlier and were derived from the first-order diagrams). Reproduce the $k^{\sigma}$ part of the representative recursion relation that equates the $k^{\sigma}$ coefficients on left and right after the RG step: $$ \bigg(\frac{b}{T}\bigg)'k^{\sigma} =2^{2-\sigma}\eta\left(\frac{1}{2^{\sigma}}\frac{b}{T}k^{\sigma}+\cdots\right), $$ where ellipses denote terms that do not produce additional leading-order $k^{\sigma}$ dependence at the order treated; this identification follows from isolating the $k^{\sigma}$ power in the diagrammatic expansions appearing in the recursion relations.

Assume the long-range fixed point is characterized by a nonzero long-range coupling at the fixed point, $b^{*}\neq0$, and enforce the fixed-point condition that the coefficient of $k^{\sigma}$ must be invariant under the RG transformation. Equating the $k^{\sigma}$ coefficients on both sides of the recursion relation and solving for $\eta$ gives, to the order in which $2-\sigma=O(\epsilon)$ is treated, the exact relation $$ \eta_{\mathrm{LR}}=2-\sigma. $$ This equality is obtained by requiring that the rescaling factor produced by the RG exactly compensates the engineering scaling of the $k^{\sigma}$ term at the fixed point, which yields $\eta=2-\sigma$.

Record the interpretation: the long-range fixed point enforces that the small-wavevector dispersion exponent (the power of $k$ governing the kinetic term, $\sigma$) and the anomalous dimension $\eta$ satisfy the scaling relation $ \sigma = 2 - \eta$, or equivalently $\eta_{\mathrm{LR}}=2-\sigma$, so that the fixed point's two-point function scaling is consistent with the dominant $k^{\sigma}$ kinetic term.

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: When the long-range coupling $b\neq0$ drives the RG flow to a long-range (LR) fixed point in the d=2+ϵ low-temperature expansion, the anomalous-dimension exponent $\eta$ at that LR fixed point is found exactly (to the order in which $2-\sigma=O(ϵ)$ is treated) to be $$ \eta_{\mathrm{LR}} \;=\; 2-\sigma, $$ i.e., the LR fixed point enforces the scaling relation that the small-wavevector dispersion exponent equals $2-\eta$.

reasoning.step: Begin from the SR fixed-point results established earlier and treated as known: $b^{*}=0$, $(T/a)^{*}=2\pi\epsilon/(n-2)$, and $\eta_{\mathrm{SR}}=\epsilon/(n-2)$ for $d=2+\epsilon$ and $n>2$ (these values were derived from the recursion relations in the low-temperature RG). These upstream results are taken as input for the eigenvalue analysis of thermal perturbations about the SR fixed point.

Specialize the recursion relations for $(a/T)$ and $(b/T)$ to the case $b=0$ to obtain the RG transformation for the combination $(T/a)$. Eliminating $\eta$ between the two recursion-relation forms and evaluating to first order in $\epsilon$ gives the flow equation for $(T/a)$: $$ \left(\frac{T}{a}\right)'=2^{-\epsilon}\left(\frac{T}{a}\right)\left(1+(n-2)\frac{T}{a}\frac{\ln2}{2\pi}\right), $$ which is the explicit multiplicative RG transformation of $(T/a)$ valid to the order treated when $b$ is held zero.

Define the correlation-length exponent $
u$ through the RG eigenvalue relation for the thermal perturbation (the deviation of $(T/a)$ from its fixed-point value): $$ \left( rac{T}{a}ight)'-\left( rac{T}{a}ight)^{*}=2^{1/
u}\Big[ rac{T}{a}-\left( rac{T}{a}ight)^{*}\Big], $$ where $(T/a)^{*}$ is the SR fixed-point value. This relation defines $2^{1/
u}$ as the linearized multiplicative eigenvalue governing flows of $(T/a)$ near the SR fixed point.

Linearize the flow equation obtained for $(T/a)$ around the fixed point $(T/a)^{*}=2\pi\epsilon/(n-2)$ by substituting $(T/a)=(T/a)^{*}+\delta(T/a)$ into the multiplicative RG equation from step 2, expanding to linear order in $\delta(T/a)$, and comparing the result with the eigenvalue definition of step 3. This comparison yields the leading-order expression for $\nu$: $$ \nu_{\mathrm{SR}}=\frac{1}{\epsilon}+O(1). $$ The authors present this as agreement with previous low-temperature calculations for the SR fixed point. [5]

conclusion.title: Correlation-length exponent at the SR fixed point (leading order)

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion:
For the SR fixed point identified in the d=2+ϵ RG analysis the correlation-length exponent $
u$ satisfies, to leading order in the small parameter $ϵ=d-2$, $$ 
u_{\mathrm{SR}} \;=\;  rac{1}{\epsilon} \;+\; O(1). $$ The exponent $
u$ is defined here by the RG eigenvalue relation for the thermal coupling combination $(T/a)$, $$ \left( rac{T}{a}ight)'\;-\;\left( rac{T}{a}ight)^{*}\;=\;2^{1/
u}\Big[ rac{T}{a}-\left( rac{T}{a}ight)^{*}\Big]. $$

reasoning.step: State the starting point taken as known from the upstream SR analysis: the SR fixed point coordinates are $b^{*}=0$, $(T/a)^{*}=2\pi\epsilon/(n-2)$, and the SR anomalous-dimension exponent is $\eta_{\mathrm{SR}}=\epsilon/(n-2)$ for $d=2+\epsilon$, $n>2$; these are used as the reference around which the long-range perturbation is linearized.

Introduce an infinitesimal long-range perturbation by adding to the Hamiltonian the term $$ \int\frac{d^{d}k}{(2\pi)^{d}}\frac{\delta b}{T}\,k^{\sigma}\,S_{\alpha}(k)S_{\alpha}(-k), $$ where $\delta b$ denotes an infinitesimal deviation (perturbation) of the long-range coefficient $b$ from zero and $S_{\alpha}(k)$ are the Fourier components of the $(n-1)$ spin fields. The object of interest is the RG multiplication factor by which $\delta b/T$ is multiplied under one RG step.

Use either of the recursion relations for $(a/T)$ and $(b/T)$ (the authors note that using either recursion relation yields the same result for this linear perturbation) and evaluate them to linear order in $\delta b/T$ about the SR fixed point $(b=0,\ (T/a)=(T/a)^{*},\ \eta=\eta_{\mathrm{SR}})$. The algebraic evaluation of the $k^{\sigma}$-term's flow yields the linearized recursion $$ \big(\delta b/T\big)' \;=\; 2^{\,2-\sigma-\eta_{\mathrm{SR}}}\;\big(\delta b/T\big). $$ This expression is the multiplicative RG eigenvalue for the long-range perturbation at the SR fixed point to the order treated.

Translate the multiplicative factor into the stability criterion: since $2^{\,2-\sigma-\eta_{\mathrm{SR}}}<1$ if and only if $2-\sigma-\eta_{\mathrm{SR}}<0$, the long-range perturbation $\delta b/T$ is irrelevant (flows to zero under repeated RG) precisely when $$ \eta_{\mathrm{SR}}>2-\sigma, $$ and is relevant (grows under RG) when $$ \eta_{\mathrm{SR}}<2-\sigma. $$ Thus the linearized multiplier $2^{2-\sigma-\eta_{\mathrm{SR}}}$ yields the explicit RG criterion separating SR stability from LR instability about the SR fixed point.

conclusion.title: Linearized RG factor for a long-range perturbation about the SR fixed point

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: Linearizing the RG recursion relations about the SR fixed point yields the multiplicative renormalization factor for an infinitesimal long-range perturbation $\delta b/T$: $$ \big(\delta b/T\big)' \;=\; 2^{\,2-\sigma-\eta_{SR}}\;\big(\delta b/T\big). $$ Consequently, the LR perturbation is irrelevant (flows to zero) under the RG when $\eta_{SR}>2-\sigma$, and relevant (grows) when $\eta_{SR}<2-\sigma$. This linear relation is the explicit RG criterion that establishes the dividing line between SR and LR domains of attraction found in conclusion 1.

reasoning.step: Declare the upstream results to be used as known starting points: the SR anomalous exponent $\eta_{\mathrm{SR}}=\epsilon/(n-2)$ (conclusion 2) and the LR fixed-point anomalous dimension $\eta_{\mathrm{LR}}=2-\sigma$ (conclusion 4) are treated as given for the algebraic determination of the LR fixed-point coupling ratios. Also retain the definitions $d=2+\epsilon$, $a$ and $b$ as the coefficients of $k^{2}$ and $k^{\sigma}$ respectively, and $T$ as temperature in energy units.

Extract the $k^{\sigma}$ coefficients from the recursion relations that include $b\neq0$, and impose the fixed-point condition (invariance of the $k^{\sigma}$ coefficient under one RG step). Doing this and using the structure of the diagrammatic contributions yields the algebraic fixed-point relation for the ratio $T/(a+b)$ at the LR fixed point: $$ \Big(\frac{T}{\,a+b\,}\Big)^{*} \;=\; \frac{2\pi(d-\sigma)}{\,n-1\,}. $$ This relation is obtained by equating the $k^{\sigma}$ terms in the relevant recursion relation evaluated at the fixed point and solving for $T/(a+b)$.

Next extract the $k^{2}$ coefficients of the recursion relations and impose the fixed-point conditions while using the previously obtained value of $(T/(a+b))^{*}$ and the SR anomalous exponent $\eta_{\mathrm{SR}}$ (treated as known). Solving the algebraic relation produced by the $k^{2}$ terms yields the LR fixed-point ratio of the long-range to short-range stiffness coefficients: $$ \Big(\frac{b}{a}\Big)^{*} \;=\; \frac{n-2}{\,d-\sigma\,}\big(2-\sigma-\eta_{SR}\big). $$ This expression follows from equating the $k^{2}$ coefficients at the LR fixed point and substituting the $T/(a+b)$ value obtained in the previous step; the factor $(2-\sigma-\eta_{SR})$ appears because the difference between the LR scaling combination $2-\sigma$ and the SR anomalous dimension enters the balance between $k^{\sigma}$ and $k^{2}$ contributions.

Check internal consistency of the two-coupling RG truncation: eliminate $b^{*}$ between the relations obtained in step 2 and step 3 (i.e., use $(b/a)^{*}$ from step 3 together with $(T/(a+b))^{*}$ from step 2) to derive the fixed-point value of $(T/a)^{*}$. The elimination yields $$ \left(\frac{T}{a}\right)^{*} \;=\; 2\pi(2-\sigma), $$ which coincides with the value obtained directly from the separate $k^{2}$ recursion relation when the LR fixed-point substitutions are applied. Thus the three algebraic relations for $(T/(a+b))^{*}$, $(b/a)^{*}$, and $(T/a)^{*}$ are mutually consistent to the order of approximation used, demonstrating that the truncated two-coupling RG closes and yields compatible fixed-point coordinates.

conclusion.title: LR fixed-point coupling ratios and consistency relations

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: At the LR fixed point in the d=2+ϵ analysis the temperature-to-total-stiffness ratio and the ratio of the $k^{\sigma}$ to $k^{2}$ coefficients are given, to leading order in small parameters, by the relations $$ \Big(\frac{T}{\,a+b\,}\Big)^{*} \;=\; \frac{2\pi(d-\sigma)}{\,n-1\,}, $$ $$ \Big(\frac{b}{a}\Big)^{*} \;=\; \frac{n-2}{\,d-\sigma\,}\big(2-\sigma-\eta_{SR}\big), $$ and the recursion relations are self-consistent in that elimination of $b^{*}$ from these expressions also yields $$ \left(\frac{T}{a}\right)^{*} \;=\; 2\pi(2-\sigma). $$ Here $d=2+\epsilon$, $n>2$, $a$ and $b$ are the coefficients of the $k^{2}$ and $k^{\sigma}$ terms, respectively, and $\eta_{SR}$ is the SR anomalous exponent given in conclusion 2. These algebraic relations locate the LR fixed point and demonstrate internal consistency of the two-coupling RG flow to the order treated.

reasoning.step: Begin from upstream LR fixed-point coordinates established earlier and treated as known: the LR anomalous dimension $\eta_{\mathrm{LR}}=2-\sigma$ (conclusion 4) and the LR fixed-point coupling ratios $(T/(a+b))^{*}=2\pi(d-\sigma)/(n-1)$ and $(b/a)^{*}=[(n-2)/(d-\sigma)](2-\sigma-\eta_{\mathrm{SR}})$ (conclusion 5) are used as inputs. Also retain the definitions $d=2+\epsilon$, $a$, $b$, $T$, and $\eta$.

Isolate the RG flow equation governing the thermal combination $(T/b)$ at nonzero $b$ by taking the $k^{\sigma}$ parts of the two recursion relations and eliminating $\eta$ between them. The algebraic manipulation performed by the authors yields the multiplicative flow equation for $(T/b)$ of the form $$ \left(\frac{T}{b}\right)^{\prime}=2^{-d+\sigma}\left(\frac{T}{b}\right)\left(1+(\pi-1)\frac{T}{b}\frac{b}{a+b}\frac{\ln2}{2\pi}\right), $$ where the prefactor $2^{-d+\sigma}=2^{-(d-\sigma)}$ appears as the leading scaling factor and the bracketed term represents subleading interaction corrections at the order treated.

Define the correlation-length exponent $
u$ in the LR-controlled regime by the RG eigenvalue relation for deviations of $(T/b)$ from its LR fixed-point value: $$ \left( rac{T}{b}ight)^{\prime}-\left( rac{T}{b}ight)^{*}=2^{1/
u}\Big[ rac{T}{b}-\left( rac{T}{b}ight)^{*}\Big], $$ so that $2^{1/
u}$ is the linearized multiplicative eigenvalue for thermal perturbations in the $(T/b)$ direction about the LR fixed point.

Linearize the flow equation from step 2 about the LR fixed point by substituting $(T/b)=(T/b)^{*}+\delta(T/b)$, expanding to linear order in $\delta(T/b)$, and comparing with the eigenvalue relation from step 3. The leading-order contribution to the multiplicative eigenvalue is $2^{-(d-\sigma)}$, so that matching gives the leading-order result $$ \nu_{\mathrm{LR}}=\frac{1}{\,d-\sigma\,}+O(1). $$ Thus the correlation-length exponent at the LR fixed point depends explicitly on $\sigma$ at leading order in the present low-temperature expansion.

conclusion.title: Correlation-length exponent at the LR fixed point (leading order)

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion:
At the LR fixed point exhibited in the d=2+ϵ RG the correlation-length exponent $
u$ has the leading-order value $$ 
u_{\mathrm{LR}} \;=\;  rac{1}{\,d-\sigma\,} \;+\; O(1), $$ where $d=2+\epsilon$ and the exponent is extracted from the RG eigenvalue of the thermal combination $(T/b)$ via $$ \left( rac{T}{b}ight)'\;-\;\left( rac{T}{b}ight)^{*}\;=\;2^{1/
u}\Big[ rac{T}{b}-\left( rac{T}{b}ight)^{*}\Big]. $$ Thus $
u_{\mathrm{LR}}$ depends explicitly on $\sigma$ (in addition to $d$ and $n$) at leading order in the present expansion.

reasoning.step: Treat as known the linearized RG multiplier for a small long-range perturbation about the SR fixed point obtained earlier: the multiplicative factor for $\delta b/T$ under a single RG step is $$ \big(\delta b/T\big)' \;=\; 2^{\,2-\sigma-\eta_{SR}}\;\big(\delta b/T\big), $$ where $\eta_{SR}$ is the SR anomalous-dimension exponent evaluated at the SR fixed point (this result is the linearization derived in the SR context and is treated as an upstream input).

Translate the multiplicative factor into a stability condition by noting that a multiplicative factor less than unity implies the perturbation is irrelevant (decays under repeated RG), while a factor greater than unity implies the perturbation is relevant (grows under repeated RG). The multiplicative factor $2^{2-\sigma-\eta_{SR}}$ is less than unity precisely when $2-\sigma-\eta_{SR}<0$, and greater than unity when $2-\sigma-\eta_{SR}>0$; equality corresponds to marginality. Therefore the dividing condition between SR-stable and LR-stable behavior is $$ \sigma \;=\; 2-\eta_{SR}. $$ This algebraic line in $(d,\sigma,n)$-space separates the two domains of attraction.

State the two alternatives implied by the stability test: (i) if $\eta_{SR}>2-\sigma$ then the long-range perturbation is irrelevant and the RG flow from initial Hamiltonians with small $b$ is attracted to the SR fixed point; hence universal critical exponents depend only on $d$ and $n$ and are independent of $\sigma$. (ii) if $\eta_{SR}<2-\sigma$ then the long-range perturbation is relevant and the SR fixed point is unstable to the LR coupling; in that case the RG flow is driven to the LR fixed point (whose anomalous dimension is $\eta_{\mathrm{LR}}=2-\sigma$) and at least the correlation-length exponent $\nu$ acquires explicit dependence on $\sigma$ in addition to $d$ and $n$. These two alternatives correspond precisely to the domains separated by the line $\sigma=2-\eta_{SR}$.

Note the internal consistency with the LR fixed-point analysis: when the LR fixed point governs criticality it enforces $\eta_{\mathrm{LR}}=2-\sigma$ (as established earlier), so at the boundary between domains the SR anomalous dimension reaches the LR value; the linearized instability calculation thereby divides $(d,\sigma,n)$-space into regions of SR attraction and LR attraction separated by the stated line. The authors summarize this division graphically and state the theorem that for $n>2$ and $d=2+\epsilon$ the LR perturbation is irrelevant when $\eta_{SR}>2-\sigma$ and relevant when $\eta_{SR}<2-\sigma$. Fig. 2

conclusion.title: Dividing-line theorem for LR versus SR critical behavior near d=2

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: For classical vector ferromagnets with n>2 components, spatial dimension d=2+ϵ with small ϵ>0, and two-body exchange whose Fourier-space small-k dispersion contains both a short-range $k^{2}$ term with coefficient $a$ and a long-range $k^{\sigma}$ term with coefficient $b$, the following dividing-line statement holds at the level of the low-temperature (d=2+ϵ) renormalization-group (RG) analysis carried out to first order in $ϵ$ and to leading order in $2-\sigma=O(ϵ)$: define $\eta_{SR}$ as the anomalous-dimension exponent at the short-range fixed point (the exponent entering the field rescaling $S'(\vec{q}')=2^{-(d+2-\eta)/2}S(\vec{q})$). Then the RG flow gives two distinct stable fixed points and domains of attraction separated by the line $$ \sigma \;=\; 2-\eta_{SR}. $$ If $\eta_{SR}>2-\sigma$ the long-range perturbation is irrelevant and the critical behavior is governed by the short-range fixed point (critical exponents depend on $d$ and $n$ but not on $\sigma$). If $\eta_{SR}<2-\sigma$ the short-range fixed point is unstable to the long-range coupling and the critical behavior is governed by a long-range fixed point with $\eta=\eta_{LR}=2-\sigma$ and with at least the correlation-length exponent $\nu$ acquiring explicit dependence on $\sigma$ in addition to $d$ and $n$.
